title: Disney Villainous
aliases: Disney Villainous: The Worst Takes It All

subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Exact components shown in the provided rules excerpt: 6 Villain Movers, 6 Villain Decks (30 cards in each deck), 6 Fate Decks (15 cards in each deck), 6 Villain Guides, 6 Boards, 6 Reference Cards, 3 Lock Tokens, 1 Fate Token, 80 Power Tokens, and 1 Cauldron. Each player also uses one villain-specific set: matching Board, Villain Mover, Villain deck, Fate deck, Villain Guide, and Reference Card.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Each player creates a personal play area: 1) Place your villain board in front of you (each board has four locations). 2) Put your Villain Mover on the left-most location. 3) If your right-most location has a lock symbol, place a Lock Token on that location. 4) Place your Villain Deck face down to the left of your board with room for a discard pile. 5) Place your Fate Deck face down to the right of your board with room for a Fate discard pile. 6) Place the Cauldron with all 80 Power tokens in a central area reachable by all players.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player chooses one villain and takes the corresponding Board, Villain Mover, Villain Deck, Fate Deck, Villain Guide, and Reference Card. Shuffle your Villain Deck and draw a starting hand of 4 cards. Keep your hand secret from other players. Shuffle your Fate Deck and keep it face down in your own area. Confirm that all players have read their objective aloud from their board so the table understands each win condition.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: Choose a first player. Starting Power is assigned by seating order from first player: 1st player gets 0 Power, 2nd gets 1 Power, 3rd and 4th each get 2 Power, 5th and 6th each get 3 Power. Place these tokens from the central Cauldron into each player’s personal supply. The first player begins turn sequence by moving their Villain Mover to a legal location and performing available actions there.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
A player turn follows this flow:
1. Move your Villain Mover to a different location in your realm (you cannot stay where you are).
2. Perform the visible action symbols at that location in any order.
3. Resolve card effects and any triggered abilities from actions you took.
4. Draw back up to 4 cards from your Villain Deck at end of turn (if below 4).
5. Check win condition timing (some villains can only win at the start of their turn).

After your turn, play passes clockwise. The game ends immediately when a player fulfills their villain’s objective according to that objective’s timing.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
The reference/action symbols shown in the excerpt define core actions:
- Gain Power: Take Power tokens from the Cauldron equal to the symbol amount.
- Play a Card: Pay the printed Power cost from your supply and play a card from hand to a legal target/location in your realm, following card text.
- Move an Item or Ally: Move one eligible Item or Ally in your realm as allowed by card/location restrictions.
- Move a Hero: Move one Hero within your realm as allowed.
- Activate: Use an Activated Ability on an in-play card that has one, paying any required cost.
- Vanquish: Defeat a Hero using Ally strength and vanquish rules from instructions/card text.
- Fate: Target another player with Fate effects by drawing/playing from their Fate deck according to Fate procedure.
- Discard Cards: Discard any number of cards from hand to your Villain discard pile; refill happens at end of turn.

General constraints and reminders from provided materials:
- You only get actions that are currently available at the location where your Villain Mover is standing.
- Covered action spaces (often by Heroes) are unavailable until uncovered.
- Action icons can be modified by card effects (example shown in guides: Items can grant extra actions at a location).
- Card text can create exceptions; follow card text when it conflicts with baseline procedure.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Asymmetry is the central mechanic: each villain has a unique objective and tailored deck, so strategy and timing differ dramatically between characters. The excerpt lists these objective structures:
- Captain Hook: Defeat Peter Pan at the Jolly Roger.
- Jafar: Start your turn with Magic Lamp at Sultan’s Palace and Genie under your control.
- Maleficent: Start your turn with a Curse at each location.
- Prince John: Start your turn with at least 20 Power.
- Queen of Hearts: Have a Wicket at each location and successfully take a shot.
- Ursula: Start your turn with the Trident and Crown at Ursula’s Lair.

Start-of-turn victory timing:
The reference text marks some villains with an asterisk indicating they can only win at the start of their turn. This timing matters: reaching the board state during your own turn may not win immediately if your objective requires start-of-turn verification.

Fate pressure and interaction:
Players can slow opponents by using Fate actions against them, introducing Heroes or disruptive effects into their realms. This is the primary interactive brake against a leading player.

Locks and realm progression:
Some villain boards begin with a locked right-most location. Unlocking/access timing is part of each villain’s tempo and can delay access to key action spaces.

Villain Guides:
Each guide includes character-specific strategy and card interactions (e.g., activated abilities, special Fate interactions, or objective combo sequencing). Treat guide text as core to teaching that villain, not optional flavor.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
Villainous has no victory-point scoring track. Endgame is objective-based and immediate.

End trigger:
The game ends as soon as one player fulfills their villain’s objective with correct timing (immediate or start-of-turn depending on objective text). That player wins instantly.

All win-condition sources in the provided rules excerpt:
- Captain Hook objective completion condition.
- Jafar objective completion condition.
- Maleficent objective completion condition.
- Prince John objective completion condition.
- Queen of Hearts objective completion condition.
- Ursula objective completion condition.

Tiebreakers:
Because the game ends immediately when a player satisfies their objective, ties are generally not used. If a table ever reaches an ambiguous simultaneous effect, resolve by standard turn order and card timing hierarchy from the instructions, then declare the first legal objective completion as winner.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Build economy first: in early turns, prioritize locations/actions that generate reliable Power and card flow, because almost every objective requires an engine before a finish.
2) Learn your objective timing window: if your villain wins at start of turn, your opening should set up a protected board state one turn ahead, not just rush flashy plays.
3) Cycle weak cards aggressively: use discard actions to improve hand quality and find objective-critical pieces sooner.
4) Watch Fate pressure from turn one: if you appear too far ahead too early, expect concentrated Fate attacks; keep backup lines ready.
5) Use your Villain Guide as a scripted early map for first games with any character—many villains have non-obvious sequencing.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Playing only for immediate value, not objective relevance. New players often spend Power on cards that do not advance the actual win condition.
2) Ignoring movement planning. Because you must move each turn, bad location sequencing can lock you out of needed actions for a full round.
3) Hoarding cards without cycling. Stale hands slow your engine and delay combo pieces.
4) Underusing Fate. If you never pressure the leader, objective races become solitaire and end abruptly against you.
5) Misreading start-of-turn objectives. Reaching the condition during your turn may not win immediately, so failing to survive one full table cycle is a common loss pattern.
6) Overcommitting resources to clearing every nuisance. Sometimes you should accept minor disruption and keep advancing your primary objective clock.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
1) Race vs disruption: decide when to spend actions on your own objective versus using Fate to delay the current leader. This balance shifts constantly by table state.
2) Power tempo vs board position: some turns should maximize Power, others should prioritize exact location/action access even with lower immediate income.
3) Card retention vs discard velocity: holding situational combo pieces can be correct, but dead hands lose games. Strong play means knowing when to keep a setup card and when to dump it to dig.
4) Threat presentation: showing obvious progress invites Fate focus. Sometimes the best line is advancing in less visible steps until you can threaten a near-unstoppable finish.

metadata.notes: Primary source: Villainous text provided in prompt (component counts, setup sequence, action symbols, objective/timing statements).
